steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The claim is that Newland 'compares his intellect favorably to that of other men' — meaning he believes he is intellectually superior. The answer must be a quotation that directly illustrates this.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'life going on': About continuity or routine, not intellectual self-comparison. ✗
  • Choice B — 'felt himself distinctly the superior... read more, thought more, seen more': Newland explicitly judges himself superior and cites intellectual reasons. Directly supports the claim. ✓
  • Choice C — 'opera setting': Describes the scene, not Newland's view of his own intellect. ✗
  • Choice D — 'accepts the men's views': Shows Newland deferring to others — the opposite of feeling superior. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
